label: Toxic Chemicals Flowing into Great Lakes Drinking Water

Toxic chemicals are flowing into the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River — and they’re showing up in drinking water.
Jaela Bernstien follows the trail of dangerous chemicals polluting a vast watershed. #Canada #USA newsinteractives.cbc.ca/features/202...
Toxic chemicals are flowing into the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River, and they’re showing up in drinking water.

Forever chemicals can last for hundreds or even thousands of years in the environment, and scientists are seeing worrying trends of forever chemicals in the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River. Check out this interactive article by CBC’s Jaela Bernstien: newsinteractives.cbc.ca/features/202...

effluent is our treated water which is all released safely to the environment (Cuyahoga River and Lake Erie). we don’t have permits for direct land application of our biosolids. but our incinerator ash is beneficially reused in soil and concrete blends.

Scientists are tracking rising levels of “forever chemicals” in the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River, where contamination threatens water for millions of people in Canada and the U.S. Almost 1/4 of Canada drinks water from the Great Lakes. #ClimateChange newsinteractives.cbc.ca/features/202...

U.S. Representatives Brian Fitzpatrick and Great Lakes Task Force Co-Chair Debbie Dingell recently introduced the PFAS National Drinking Water Standard Act, which modifies enforceable limits for PFAS under the Safe Drinking Water Act. Read more here: buff.ly/X4ou2sd

My interest in pollution and toxic waste began when I was young in Sarnia, Ontario. That's where Lake Huron outflows to the St. Clair River, which divides Michigan from Ontario, the USA from Canada. I often joked about growing up “downwind of Dow Chemical.”
